papers.title: Consumer-Driven Class Pricing
papers.abstract: This paper proposes a consumer loss aversion-based rationale for class pricing, which is the practice of assigning only a few price points to many differentiated products.

papers.title: EXPRESS: Choosing Larger Portion Sizes for Others
papers.authors: Peggy J. Liu, Theresa A. Kwon, Ignazio Ziano
papers.abstract: Consumers’ portion size choices are important, as they can influence how much food is eaten and wasted. While previous research has focused on self-selected portion sizes, this research examines portion size choices for others. The authors demonstrate that consumers choose larger portion sizes for others than for themselves, across both healthy and unhealthy foods, and for a wide range of different others. Choosing larger portion sizes for others is problematic, as the authors show that it can contribute to increased food waste. Both process-consistent moderation and mediation show that one driver of choosing larger portion sizes for others is consumers’ desire to view themselves as being caring. Alternative accounts (e.g., predicting that others want to eat more than the self, greater consumption amount uncertainty for others than the self) are addressed. Finally, a process-consistent intervention that reframes the meaning of being caring as mitigating the burden of unwanted leftovers for others decreases portion size choices for others. Altogether, this research offers theoretical contributions to the literatures on food decision-making and choices for others and offers practical contributions by identifying a novel contributor to food waste in social contexts and thus a potential target for interventions aimed at mitigating food waste.

papers.title: EXPRESS: Dual Market Navigation
papers.authors: Kellilynn M. Frias, Deidre Popovich, Ronald P. Hill
papers.abstract:
Dual market navigation refers to consumers’ ongoing engagement across structurally distinct and institutionally separate markets. While prior research has focused on how consumers use service cues to evaluate individual service providers or products, little is known about how consumers use these cues across structurally distinct markets, despite their growing prevalence. We address this gap by extending cue utilization theory to the market level, shifting attention from firm-led, market-expansion strategies to consumer-led, dual-market strategies. Drawing on qualitative data from insured consumers living and routinely shopping for healthcare along the U.S.-Mexico border, we develop the concept of
                    strategic cue auditing:
                    the sensemaking process consumers use to evaluate intrinsic and/or extrinsic cue(s) and make comparative assessments of market-level quality cues across different contexts. We identify three cue auditing strategies (reactive, cultural-relational, and cross-comparative) that reveal how consumers make sense of competing market logics when judging service and product quality. These strategies shape healthcare choices and reveal what we conceptualize as cue misfires and misalignments. By theorizing cue utilization as structurally contingent and market-level, this research advances understanding of dual-market navigation beyond healthcare and offers implications for marketers and policymakers operating in contexts including cross-border, online-offline, and formal-informal markets.
papers.analysis_ko:
네, 세계적인 마케팅 학술 연구 보조 AI로서 요청하신 논문 'EXPRESS: Dual Market Navigation'에 대해 심도 있게 분석해 드리겠습니다.


A. 논문 요약:
본 논문은 소비자들이 구조적으로 다르고 제도적으로 분리된 두 개 이상의 시장을 지속적으로 넘나드는 행위인 '이중 시장 탐색(Dual Market Navigation)'이라는 개념을 제시합니다. 기존 연구가 단일 시장 내에서 소비자들이 특정 서비스나 제품을 평가하기 위해 단서(cue)를 어떻게 활용하는지에 초점을 맞춘 반면, 이 연구는 소비자들이 서로 다른 시장 시스템 간에 단서를 어떻게 비교하고 평가하는지에 주목합니다. 미국-멕시코 국경 지역에 거주하며 양국의 의료 서비스를 모두 이용하는 보험 가입자들을 대상으로 한 질적 연구를 통해, 저자들은 '전략적 단서 감사(Strategic Cue Auditing)'라는 핵심 개념을 도출합니다. 이는 소비자들이 내재적/외재적 단서들을 비교 평가하며 시장 수준의 품질을 이해하고 판단하는 의미 형성 과정입니다. 연구 결과, 소비자들은 반응적(reactive), 문화-관계적(cultural-relational), 교차-비교적(cross-comparative)이라는 세 가지 단서 감사 전략을 사용하며, 이 과정에서 발생하는 '단서 불발(cue misfires)'과 '단서 불일치(cue misalignments)' 현상을 발견했습니다. 이 연구는 단서 활용 이론을 개별 제품 수준에서 시장 수준으로 확장하며, 국경 간 거래, 온라인-오프라인 시장, 공식-비공식 시장 등 다양한 현대적 소비 환경을 이해하는 데 중요한 시사점을 제공합니다.

B. 연구적 의의:
이 논문의 연구적 의의는 크게 세 가지 측면에서 분석할 수 있습니다.

1.  관점의 전환: 기업 중심에서 소비자 주도적 패러다임으로: 기존 마케팅 연구는 주로 기업이 어떻게 새로운 시장으로 확장하는가(firm-led, market-expansion)에 초점을 맞추었습니다. 그러나 이 논문은 소비자들이 주체적으로 여러 시장의 경계를 넘나들며 자신의 필요를 충족시키는 '소비자 주도적 이중 시장 전략(consumer-led, dual-market strategies)'에 주목함으로써 연구의 관점을 근본적으로 전환시켰습니다. 이는 현대 소비자들이 더 이상 수동적인 시장 수용자가 아니라, 자신의 목적을 위해 여러 시장 시스템을 능동적으로 활용하고 재구성하는 행위자임을 보여주는 새로운 패러다임을 제시합니다.

2.  기존 이론의 확장 및 현대화: 본 연구는 고전적인 '단서 활용 이론(Cue Utilization Theory)'을 현대의 복잡한 소비 환경에 맞게 '시장 수준(market-level)'으로 확장했습니다. 기존 이론이 주로 가격, 브랜드, 원산지 같은 단서를 통해 단일 제품의 품질을 추론하는 미시적 과정에 머물렀다면, 이 연구는 소비자들이 의료 시스템의 전문성, 문화적 이해도, 행정 절차 등 거시적인 '시장 수준의 단서'를 비교 평가하는 과정을 이론화했습니다. 이는 이론의 설명력을 글로벌화, 디지털화된 현대 시장 환경으로 확장시킨 중요한 공헌입니다.

3.  현 시대적 상황과의 높은 관련성: 논문에서 다루는 '이중 시장 탐색'은 특정 지역의 특수한 현상이 아니라, 현대 소비자들이 일상적으로 경험하는 보편적 행위입니다. 예를 들어, 소비자들은 온라인 쇼핑몰과 오프라인 매장을 비교하며 구매 결정을 내리고(온라인-오프라인 시장), 정식 유통 채널과 중고 거래 플랫폼을 동시에 활용하며(공식-비공식 시장), 해외 직구를 통해 국내 시장과 해외 시장의 상품을 비교합니다(국경 간 시장). 이 논문은 이러한 복합적인 소비 행동의 기저에 있는 심리적, 행동적 메커니즘을 '전략적 단서 감사'라는 개념으로 명쾌하게 설명함으로써, 다양한 현대 마케팅 현상을 통합적으로 이해할 수 있는 강력한 분석 틀을 제공합니다.

C. 저자 백그라운드:
제가 아는 정보를 바탕으로 저자들의 학문적 배경을 설명해 드리겠습니다.

1.  Kellilynn M. Frias: 텍사스 테크 대학교(Texas Tech University)의 마케팅 조교수입니다. 그녀의 주요 연구 분야는 시장 형성(market creation), 소비자 문화 이론(consumer culture theory), 사회적 기업가 정신, 그리고 소외된 소비자(underserved consumers)입니다. 특히 사회 구조적으로 불리한 위치에 있는 소비자들이 어떻게 시장에 참여하고 가치를 창출하는지에 깊은 관심을 가지고 있으며, 본 논문의 연구 맥락(국경 지역의 의료 서비스 소비)은 그녀의 핵심 연구 관심사와 정확히 일치합니다.

2.  Deidre Popovich: 텍사스 테크 대학교(Texas Tech University)의 마케팅 조교수입니다. 주요 연구 분야는 소비자 웰빙(consumer well-being), 전환적 소비자 연구(Transformative Consumer Research, TCR), 헬스케어 마케팅 등입니다. 소비자의 삶의 질을 향상시키는 마케팅의 역할에 대해 연구하며, 특히 헬스케어 분야에서 소비자들이 겪는 어려움과 의사결정 과정에 대한 전문가입니다. Frias 교수와 함께 헬스케어와 소외 계층이라는 공통의 연구 주제를 공유하고 있습니다.

3.  Ronald P. Hill: 아메리칸 대학교(American University)의 마케팅 및 경영학 석좌교수이자, 마케팅 분야에서 세계적으로 저명한 학자입니다. 그는 수십 년간 빈곤, 사회적 약자, 마케팅 윤리, 기업의 사회적 책임, 전환적 소비자 연구(TCR) 분야를 개척해 온 대가입니다. 그의 연구는 마케팅이 사회에 미치는 긍정적, 부정적 영향을 깊이 탐구하는 것으로 유명하며, 본 논문에서 이론적 깊이와 방향성을 제시하는 데 핵심적인 역할을 했을 것으로 보입니다. 세 저자의 조합은 소외 계층의 헬스케어 소비라는 구체적인 현상을 소비자 웰빙과 마케팅의 사회적 책임이라는 거시적인 이론적 틀 안에서 분석하는 최적의 팀이라고 할 수 있습니다.
papers.analysis_en:
A. Summary:
This paper introduces the concept of "Dual Market Navigation," which refers to consumers' ongoing engagement across structurally distinct and institutionally separate markets. While prior research focused on how consumers use cues to evaluate a single service or product within one market, this study examines how consumers compare and assess cues across different market systems. Through a qualitative study of insured consumers living and seeking healthcare along the U.S.-Mexico border, the authors develop the core concept of "Strategic Cue Auditing." This is the sensemaking process consumers use to comparatively assess intrinsic and extrinsic cues to judge market-level quality. The study identifies three cue auditing strategies: reactive, cultural-relational, and cross-comparative. It also uncovers phenomena termed "cue misfires" and "cue misalignments" that occur during this process. This research extends cue utilization theory from the product level to the market level, offering significant implications for understanding various modern consumption contexts, such as cross-border, online-offline, and formal-informal markets.

B. Academic Significance:
The academic significance of this paper can be analyzed from three main perspectives:

1.  Paradigm Shift: From Firm-Centric to Consumer-Led: Traditional marketing research has predominantly focused on firm-led strategies, such as how companies expand into new markets. This paper fundamentally shifts the perspective by focusing on "consumer-led, dual-market strategies," where consumers proactively navigate across market boundaries to fulfill their needs. It proposes a new paradigm that views modern consumers not as passive recipients within a market, but as active agents who strategically utilize and reconfigure multiple market systems for their own purposes.

2.  Extension and Modernization of Existing Theory: The study extends the classic Cue Utilization Theory to fit the complex contemporary consumption landscape by elevating it to the "market level." While the original theory was largely confined to the micro-level process of inferring a single product's quality from cues like price or brand, this research theorizes how consumers evaluate macro-level "market cues" such as the professionalism of a healthcare system, cultural attunement, or administrative processes. This is a significant contribution that enhances the theory's explanatory power in a globalized and digitized market environment.

3.  High Relevance to Contemporary Contexts: The concept of "dual market navigation" is not a niche phenomenon but a universal behavior experienced by modern consumers daily. For instance, consumers make purchasing decisions by comparing online stores and offline retailers (online-offline markets), utilize both official distribution channels and secondhand platforms like P2P marketplaces (formal-informal markets), and compare domestic and international products through cross-border e-commerce. This paper provides a powerful analytical framework by clearly articulating the underlying psychological and behavioral mechanism of this complex behavior with the concept of "strategic cue auditing," enabling a more integrated understanding of diverse modern marketing phenomena.

C. Author Background:
Based on my knowledge, here is the academic background of the authors:

1.  Kellilynn M. Frias: An Assistant Professor of Marketing at Texas Tech University. Her primary research interests include market creation, consumer culture theory, social entrepreneurship, and underserved consumers. She is particularly interested in how consumers in structurally disadvantaged positions participate in and create value from markets. The research context of this paper (healthcare consumption in a border region) aligns perfectly with her core research interests.

2.  Deidre Popovich: An Assistant Professor of Marketing at Texas Tech University. Her main research areas are consumer well-being, Transformative Consumer Research (TCR), and healthcare marketing. She studies the role of marketing in improving consumers' quality of life and is an expert on the challenges and decision-making processes consumers face, especially in the healthcare sector. She shares common research themes of healthcare and underserved populations with Dr. Frias.

3.  Ronald P. Hill: A Professor and Dean's Chair in Marketing and Business Law at American University, and a world-renowned scholar in the field of marketing. He is a leading figure who has pioneered research in poverty, vulnerable populations, marketing ethics, corporate social responsibility, and Transformative Consumer Research (TCR) for decades. His work is famous for deeply exploring the positive and negative societal impacts of marketing. He likely played a key role in providing the theoretical depth and direction for this paper. The combination of these three authors represents an ideal team to analyze the specific phenomenon of healthcare consumption among an underserved population within a broader theoretical framework of consumer well-being and marketing's societal role.

papers.title: EXPRESS: High Tech, Not Low Touch: Empowering the B2B Sales Force through Online Channel Integration
papers.authors: Irene Nahm, Phillip Wiseman, Michael Ahearne, Seshadri Tirunillai
papers.abstract: As many B2B companies are transforming their sales processes by adding online channels to the sales force, it is critical to better understand how salespeople respond, adapt, and manage relationships after customers adopt them. Leveraging data from a large B2B firm, we investigate how customers’ integration of an online channel affects salesperson-customer relationships. We find that customer online channel integration leads to a 7% increase in quarterly customer gross margins, with 28% of a customer's sales volume shifting online, but only a 9% reduction in face-to-face sales transactions relative to that of before the online channel integration. Salespeople continue to hold a substantial number of face-to-face meetings to handle transactions and engage in other activities with customers that integrate the online channel than expected, given the proportion of sales volume that those customers shift online. Relatedly, customer integration of the online channel enhances the quality of salesperson-customer interaction within those relationships, reducing products sold at a loss and increasing the breadth of product categories sold. We also identify important contingency factors regarding online channel integration, including that its effectiveness when use is highly synchronized with face-to-face sales transaction meetings can further drive customer gross margins by about 16%.

papers.title: EXPRESS: Measuring Heterogeneity in TV Advertising Elasticities: Evidence from 135 Retail and Restaurant Brands
papers.authors: Samsun Knight, Tsung-Yiou Hsieh, Yakov Bart
papers.abstract: We estimate the heterogeneity of TV advertising effectiveness across store characteristics and advertising levels using a large-scale panel of 135 US retail and restaurant brands, and then use these estimates to assess strategies for improving TV advertising performance. We find significant heterogeneity in TV advertising elasticity across characteristics for over 93% of brands, but show that firms’ observed allocations generally fail to fully exploit this estimated heterogeneity and instead covary much more closely with simple heuristics. For example, we find that firms tend to advertise in areas where they already have high revenue, rather than in the areas estimated to have the highest incremental revenue from advertising. In particular, brands tend to overinvest in dense, high-income markets and underinvest in markets with high concentrations of college-educated residents. We project that brands could improve ad lift by a median 2.35 percentage points (relative to <0.5% median baseline ad lift) and earn tens of millions in additional revenue under identical-budget reallocations that better leverage this heterogeneity, and that 14-16 percentage points of brands with negative return-on-investment (ROI) from TV advertising could achieve positive ROI through such reallocations.

papers.title: EXPRESS: The Making-Up-For-Failure Nudge: Framing Subgoals as Opportunities for Redemption Increases Goal Persistence
papers.authors: Shannon M. Duncan, Marissa A. Sharif
papers.abstract:
Small failures during goal pursuit are inevitable and often derail consumers from reaching their overall goals. This research demonstrates one simple, cost-free nudge: encouraging consumers to make up for small failures. The making-up-for-failure nudge encourages more goal-consistent action today to make up for failing to reach one’s goal yesterday. This leads consumers to perceive the day after a failure as an opportunity to redeem themselves, encoding missing this opportunity as
                    two
                    goal failures (both the previously failed subgoal and the current subgoal) rather than
                    one
                    (just the current subgoal). As a result, consumers anticipate feeling worse about missing their opportunity to redeem themselves than failing a subgoal itself. To avoid the heightened negative anticipated emotion of failing
                    not one, but two
                    , subgoals, consumers are motivated to engage in more goal-consistent behavior after failure. This effect is documented in two longitudinal real-behavior studies, four real-behavior lab studies, and one hypothetical study across various domains (i.e., learning a new language, exercising, and word search games). Three relevant boundary conditions are identified, including 1) how people feel after failure, 2) difficulty/ease of making up for failure, and 3) when people are encouraged to make up for their failure.

papers.title: From Adaptation to Disruption: Structured Ambivalence as a Catalyst for Consumer-Led Institutional Work
papers.abstract:
Abstract
                  This study explores how consumers respond to the ambivalence sparked by conflicting institutional norms in contexts such as the United States market for donor conception (i.e. egg, sperm, embryo). While prior research has framed responses to ambivalence as “coping,” we theorize these responses as consumer-led institutional work—i.e., active efforts to adapt to or challenge institutional norms. Drawing on interviews, archival narratives, and case profiles, we develop a typology distinguishing between adaptation work (efforts to reconcile contradictions in existing institutional arrangements) and disruption work (efforts to transform institutional arrangements), with individual and collaborative variants of each. Theoretically, we argue that structured ambivalence is both the experience through which institutional contradictions become salient and the catalyst that motivates institutional work. Our contributions are twofold. First, we demonstrate that adaptive work, which facilitates individual participation, paradoxically reinforces the very market system that produces ambivalence. Second, we identify structured ambivalence as a micro-level emotional catalyst driving the full spectrum of consumer-led action, ranging from quiet adaptation to the creation of new, market-changing organizations. This provides a more nuanced understanding of why consumers engage in institutional work, contributing to the microfoundations of institutions perspective by specifying the emotional and reflexive underpinnings of institutional continuity and change within markets.

papers.title: Leveraging Large-Scale Granular Single-Source Data for TV Advertising: An Identification Strategy
papers.authors: Tsung-Yiou Hsieh, Rex Yuxing Du, Shijie Lu
papers.abstract: This paper introduces a new instrumental variable to estimate the causal effects of linear TV advertising, using granular household-level viewing and purchase panel data.

papers.title: More Correlations Signal Causation: The Effect of Correlational Scope on Perceived Causality
papers.abstract:
Abstract
                  In the era of big data, an increasing amount of information is becoming available to business analysts and scientists. Statistical correlations between consumption patterns and individual conditions (e.g., health conditions) are frequently uncovered and reported in the media. However, many correlations are spurious, prompting the question of when consumers perceive them as reflecting causal relationships. Across eight preregistered studies, we demonstrate that a correlation (e.g., between drinking tea and bone health) is perceived as more likely to reflect a causal relationship (i.e., drinking tea makes bones healthier) when the plausible cause reportedly correlates with additional outcomes (e.g., heart conditions). The correlational scope effect is attenuated when the additional outcomes are perceived as weakly related to the focal outcome, mitigated under a cause-last framing (in which the plausible cause in a correlation is presented after the target outcome), and can influence product choices. We propose that category-based induction may contribute to the correlational scope effect: people project the perceived susceptibility to a cause from the additional outcomes onto the focal outcome. These findings have implications for our understanding of causal judgment and for consumers’ well-being.
